content.text: Noted: a per-machine "machine-specific packages" list, never synced, populated by three-way prompts (skip once / skip always / apply), covering every manager — not just apt. Follow-ups on that:

questions.question: Where should the per-machine list live so it can never be synced?

options.label: ~/.local/share/pc-switcher/ (Recommended)
options.description: Machine-locality comes for free: ADR-017 already hard-excludes `.local/share/pc-switcher` from folder_sync (non-overridable, emitted before any user filter), and it's outside config.yaml so config_sync never touches it. It sits next to sync-history.json, which is exactly the same kind of per-machine state. Zero new exclusion machinery.

options.label: ~/.config/pc-switcher/ next to config.yaml
options.description: Discoverable — users look for pc-switcher settings there. But that directory is inside /home and IS mirrored by folder_sync, so the file would need a new non-overridable exclusion, and it sits confusingly beside the file that explicitly IS synced.

options.label: /etc or another root-owned location
options.description: Unambiguously machine-scoped and outside /home entirely. But it lands in Phase 3's /etc territory and needs sudo to edit — friction for a list the user curates by hand.

questions.question: What exactly does an entry in machine M's list mean?

options.label: Inert on M, in both roles (Recommended)
options.description: One rule: a package listed on M is untouched whenever M is involved — not pushed when M is the source, not removed or installed when M is the target. So P17 lists `brscan3` and it neither propagates to the laptop nor gets removed from P17 by a reverse sync. Each 'skip always' answer is recorded on the machine where the decision was about.

options.label: Two lists: don't-push and don't-install
options.description: Separate a package I own but shouldn't share (brscan3 on P17) from one I never want here (nvidia stack on the laptop). More precise, but two lists to maintain and two prompt variants — and the single-rule version already covers both once each machine has answered once.

options.label: Inert on M as target only
options.description: Protects M from unwanted installs/removals, but a machine-specific package still gets pushed when M is the source, so the peer has to answer the same prompt in the other direction.

questions.question: How are these decisions presented during a sync?

options.label: One batched review before any change (Recommended)
options.description: The job computes the full diff, then presents it grouped by manager and action (install / remove) with a per-package choice of apply / skip-once / skip-always. One interaction point instead of dozens, and it satisfies 'detected and reported before any destructive change' literally. Fits the existing single persistent Live panel with pause/resume around prompts.

options.label: Prompt per package as encountered
options.description: Simplest to implement and clear cause-and-effect, but a first sync between two real machines could produce dozens of prompts with changes already partly applied — exactly the flooding problem the Phase 1 UAT round fixed.

options.label: No prompt — report, and require a second run
options.description: First run writes a proposed change list and exits; the user edits the machine-specific list, then re-runs to apply. Fully non-interactive, but breaks the single-command workflow (REQ-manual-sync-workflow).

questions.question: What happens when there's no TTY (CI, scripted run) or the user passes a non-interactive flag?

options.label: Apply installs, skip removals, report (Recommended)
options.description: Non-destructive default: additive changes go through, every removal is skipped and listed in the summary as needing a decision. Nothing is lost unattended, and the sync still makes progress — consistent with 'warn and confirm, never hard-abort'.

options.label: Skip everything unresolved, report only
options.description: Any package needing a decision is left alone; only unambiguous changes apply. Most conservative, but a first sync would achieve almost nothing headlessly.

options.label: Abort the job
options.description: Refuse to proceed without a human. Safest reading of reliability-first, but it makes the package job unusable in the VM integration tests without extra flags.

options.description: Machine-locality comes for free: ADR-017 already hard-excludes `.local/share/pc-switcher` from folder_sync (non-overridable, emitted before any user filter), and it's outside config.yaml so config_sync never touches it. It sits next to sync-history.json, which is exactly the same kind of per-machine state. Zero new exclusion machinery.
options.label: ~/.local/share/pc-switcher/ (Recommended)

options.description: Discoverable — users look for pc-switcher settings there. But that directory is inside /home and IS mirrored by folder_sync, so the file would need a new non-overridable exclusion, and it sits confusingly beside the file that explicitly IS synced.
options.label: ~/.config/pc-switcher/ next to config.yaml

options.description: Unambiguously machine-scoped and outside /home entirely. But it lands in Phase 3's /etc territory and needs sudo to edit — friction for a list the user curates by hand.
options.label: /etc or another root-owned location

questions.question: Where should the per-machine list live so it can never be synced?

options.description: One rule: a package listed on M is untouched whenever M is involved — not pushed when M is the source, not removed or installed when M is the target. So P17 lists `brscan3` and it neither propagates to the laptop nor gets removed from P17 by a reverse sync. Each 'skip always' answer is recorded on the machine where the decision was about.
options.label: Inert on M, in both roles (Recommended)

options.description: Separate a package I own but shouldn't share (brscan3 on P17) from one I never want here (nvidia stack on the laptop). More precise, but two lists to maintain and two prompt variants — and the single-rule version already covers both once each machine has answered once.
options.label: Two lists: don't-push and don't-install

options.description: Protects M from unwanted installs/removals, but a machine-specific package still gets pushed when M is the source, so the peer has to answer the same prompt in the other direction.
options.label: Inert on M as target only

questions.question: What exactly does an entry in machine M's list mean?

options.description: The job computes the full diff, then presents it grouped by manager and action (install / remove) with a per-package choice of apply / skip-once / skip-always. One interaction point instead of dozens, and it satisfies 'detected and reported before any destructive change' literally. Fits the existing single persistent Live panel with pause/resume around prompts.
options.label: One batched review before any change (Recommended)

options.description: Simplest to implement and clear cause-and-effect, but a first sync between two real machines could produce dozens of prompts with changes already partly applied — exactly the flooding problem the Phase 1 UAT round fixed.
options.label: Prompt per package as encountered

options.description: First run writes a proposed change list and exits; the user edits the machine-specific list, then re-runs to apply. Fully non-interactive, but breaks the single-command workflow (REQ-manual-sync-workflow).
options.label: No prompt — report, and require a second run

questions.question: How are these decisions presented during a sync?

options.description: Non-destructive default: additive changes go through, every removal is skipped and listed in the summary as needing a decision. Nothing is lost unattended, and the sync still makes progress — consistent with 'warn and confirm, never hard-abort'.
options.label: Apply installs, skip removals, report (Recommended)

options.description: Any package needing a decision is left alone; only unambiguous changes apply. Most conservative, but a first sync would achieve almost nothing headlessly.
options.label: Skip everything unresolved, report only

options.description: Refuse to proceed without a human. Safest reading of reliability-first, but it makes the package job unusable in the VM integration tests without extra flags.
options.label: Abort the job

questions.question: What happens when there's no TTY (CI, scripted run) or the user passes a non-interactive flag?

answers.How are these decisions presented during: batched. would be cool with a nice TUI element where I can check off items on a list.
answers.What exactly does an entry in machine M': Inert on M, in both roles (Recommended)
answers.What happens when there's no TTY (CI, sc: In normal usage: skip all once.  If you need special functionality just for testing, do it, but hide it (undocumented for user, not in --help, ...) and only make it active when a specific testing env var is set

content.text:
Locked. Note for the plan: `~/.config/pc-switcher/` **is** inside folder_sync's mirror, so that file needs its own non-overridable exclusion alongside the ADR-017 runtime-state one.

Let's talk about **Repo sources & PPAs vs. the Phase 3 boundary** — you have 21 source entries and keys in two different directories, and you just said version-constraint config should be synced.

questions.question: Packages can't install without their repos. How does Phase 2 get /etc/apt content onto the target?

options.label: Pull the apt subset into Phase 2 (Recommended)
options.description: Phase 2 owns `/etc/apt/**` (sources.list.d, preferences.d, keyrings, trusted.gpg.d, apt.conf.d) as part of package management — the same precedent as pulling /root into Phase 1 because rsync had to run as root anyway. Phase 3 then owns the rest of /etc. Requires a ROADMAP/REQUIREMENTS boundary update, exactly like Phase 1 did.

options.label: Declarative repos in the manifest
options.description: The manifest records repo definitions (URI, suite, components, signing key fingerprint, architectures) and the target reconstructs the files rather than copying them. Avoids touching /etc as a filesystem at all, and repos become reviewable data — but it's a parser + writer for deb822 and legacy one-line formats, and it will lose hand-edited nuances (your `.save`/`.curtin.orig` files show real local history).

options.label: Wait for Phase 3 — Phase 2 only reports
options.description: Phase 2 detects that a required repo is missing on the target and reports it, but the user fixes it manually until Phase 3 syncs /etc. Keeps the boundary clean, but success criterion 2 ('custom PPAs are reproduced on the target') would not be met.

questions.question: If Phase 2 handles /etc/apt files, which mechanism moves them?

options.label: Reuse folder_sync's rsync machinery on a scoped path set (Recommended)
options.description: Same transport, same metadata preservation, same filter-file authoring, same dry-run itemization — already proven in Phase 1 and running as root on both ends. Phase 2 just supplies the paths and their exclusions rather than inventing a second file-transfer path.

options.label: Purpose-built copy in the package job
options.description: The package job reads the files over SSH and writes them itself, with package-aware validation (e.g. verify a key matches the fingerprint the repo expects). More control, more code, and a second file-transfer implementation to keep correct.

options.label: Configure a folder_sync entry instead
options.description: Just add `/etc/apt` as another folder_sync folder in config. Zero new code, but it decouples repo state from the package job that depends on it — ordering and failure handling become the user's problem.

questions.question: Signing keys: your keys are split between /etc/apt/keyrings (3) and /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d (5, incl. a dated Spotify key).

options.label: Copy both directories verbatim (Recommended)
options.description: Whatever made the source's apt work makes the target's apt work. Re-fetching keys from vendors means network dependency, changed vendor URLs, and a silently different trust set — unacceptable in a reliability-first tool. Keys are small and their file metadata matters.

options.label: Copy keyrings/, migrate trusted.gpg.d/
options.description: Copy the modern per-repo keyrings, and normalize the legacy global trusted.gpg.d entries into per-repo keyrings on the way. Better end state (global trust is deprecated), but it's a migration of your machines, not a sync — and a wrong migration breaks apt.

options.label: Copy keys, and report trust-set differences
options.description: Verbatim copy, plus a report when the two machines' trust sets differ before the sync — so silent divergence in what a machine trusts is visible.

questions.question: Snap channels and flatpak remotes need to exist on the target too. In scope?

options.label: Yes — provision remotes/channels as part of the manifest (Recommended)
options.description: The manifest records flatpak remotes (name, URL, gpg key, per-scope) and snap channel tracking; the target adds any missing remote before installing. Without it, a flatpak install of a flathub app fails on a machine that never added flathub — and you have remotes in both user and system scope.

options.label: Assume remotes exist; fail with a clear message
options.description: Validate that every required remote/channel exists on the target during validate() and report precisely what's missing, but let the user add it. Smaller scope, one more manual step on a fresh machine.

options.label: Provision flathub only, report the rest
options.description: Auto-add the common case, report anything exotic. Pragmatic middle ground.

answers.If Phase 2 handles /etc/apt files, which: reuse folder_sync, but there are possible differences between machines here as well, e.g. sources for machine-specific packages. Or YAGNI?
answers.Packages can't install without their rep: Pull the apt subset into Phase 2 (Recommended)
answers.Signing keys: your keys are split betwee: copy both verbatim? Or merge them first. Just like the apt (and flatpak) sources will need to be merged on first sync (or all syncs?) rather than overwritten. I think we need a merge functionality at least on first run. Or same sync/skip once/skip always functionality and recording for sources and for keys as for packages?
answers.Snap channels and flatpak remotes need t: yes, this is equivalent to apt sources and keys

content.text: You're right, and it changes the answer to the mechanism question — a `--delete` mirror of `/etc/apt` would wipe the target's own machine-specific sources, which contradicts what you just asked for everywhere else. The consistent model is to treat repos, keys and remotes as **inventory items in the same manifest**, with the same three-way decision, rather than as a folder mirror.

questions.question: Given that, how are apt sources / keys / flatpak remotes / snap channels handled?

options.label: Inventory items, same 3-way decision as packages (Recommended)
options.description: Each repo, key, and remote is an item in the manifest with an identity (e.g. deb822 URI+suite, or remote name+URL). The target diffs its own set, and every difference gets apply / skip-once / skip-always with skip-always recorded in the same machine-specific file. Then 'merge' isn't a special first-run mode — it's just what item-level convergence does, every run, in both directions.

options.label: File mirror with an exception list
options.description: rsync /etc/apt as a mirror, but protect paths listed in the machine-specific file from deletion. Less new code, but the exception unit is a filename rather than a repo, and a repo's definition, its key, and its pin live in three different files — so one logical decision means three exceptions.

options.label: Union-only: add missing, never remove
options.description: Repos and keys only ever accumulate. Trivially safe and 'merge' is automatic, but stale repos live forever on both machines and a removed PPA never actually goes away.

questions.question: How does a repo's signing key travel?

options.label: Bundled with its repo item, verbatim (Recommended)
options.description: The item is 'this repo + the keyring file it points at', and the key file is copied byte-for-byte with its metadata. One decision covers both, so you can't end up with a source that has no trusted key. Legacy /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d keys, which no repo points at explicitly, form a separate global-trust item set.

options.label: Separate key inventory
options.description: Keys are their own item class with their own decisions, independent of repos. Handles the legacy global-trust keys uniformly, but lets you approve a repo while declining its key — a broken state the tool would then have to detect.

options.label: Bundle keys, re-fetch nothing, report trust diffs
options.description: As recommended, plus an explicit report whenever the two machines' overall trust sets differ — visibility on what each machine trusts, since that's security-relevant state.

questions.question: What about the rest of /etc/apt — preferences.d (your version pins) and apt.conf.d?

options.label: preferences.d as items; apt.conf.d machine-local (Recommended)
options.description: Pins and version constraints are policy about packages, so they sync as items (you asked for version-constraint config to travel). apt.conf.d is largely machine/vendor-generated behavioral config (proxies, periodic updates, ESM hooks) and stays local — syncing it risks pushing one machine's unattended-upgrade or proxy behavior onto the other.

options.label: Both as items
options.description: Full /etc/apt convergence with per-item decisions. Most complete replication; more prompts on first sync, and some apt.conf.d files are written by packages themselves so they'd churn.

options.label: preferences.d only; ignore apt.conf.d entirely
options.description: Sync pins, don't even report on apt.conf.d differences. Smallest scope, but real divergence in apt behavior stays invisible.

questions.question: Do repo/key/remote decisions and package decisions share one machine-specific file, or separate ones?

options.label: One file, sectioned by item type (Recommended)
options.description: A single `~/.config/pc-switcher/machine-local.yaml` with sections for apt packages, apt sources, apt keys, apt pins, snaps, flatpaks, flatpak remotes. One file to back up, one to exclude from folder_sync, one place to look when something isn't syncing.

options.label: One file per manager
options.description: apt.yaml, snap.yaml, flatpak.yaml. Cleaner if the job is later split per manager, but more exclusion rules and more places to check.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Leave the file layout to the planner, as long as it's machine-local, excluded from folder_sync, and human-editable.

answers.Do repo/key/remote decisions and package: one file per manager. I propose to immediately create distinct jobs per manager. Much cleaner scope both for dev and user.

content.text: Per-manager jobs it is — that also settles the granularity area. Let's nail the job set and, with the inventory in hand, the **bare-install scope** call you wanted to defer to this point.

questions.question: What's the job set, and where does the shared machinery live?

options.label: 3 jobs + a shared package-sync core (Recommended)
options.description: `apt_sync`, `snap_sync`, `flatpak_sync` as separate SyncJobs (own config section, own enable flag, own machine-local file, own progress bar), all built on a shared internal module holding the item model, the diff, the 3-way decision flow, the batched TUI checklist, and the machine-local file I/O. Each job only supplies 'how do I list, install, and remove items in my ecosystem'.

options.label: 3 fully independent jobs
options.description: No shared core; each job implements its own inventory and decision handling. Maximum isolation, but the checklist UI and machine-local file logic get written three times and drift apart.

options.label: 3 jobs now, extract the core later
options.description: Build apt_sync first, then factor out the shared pieces when snap_sync follows. Avoids designing an abstraction before there are two real users of it — at the cost of a refactor mid-phase.

questions.question: Ordering — package jobs run before or after folder_sync?

options.label: Packages first, then folder_sync (Recommended)
options.description: Apps get installed, then their data lands on top. Matters concretely for flatpak: `~/.local/share/flatpak` is excluded from folder_sync and must be provisioned by `flatpak install`, while the app's data in `~/.var/app` comes from folder_sync — installing first means the app exists before its profile arrives. Also lets a newly-installed package's postinst write defaults that folder_sync then overwrites with your real config, rather than the reverse.

options.label: folder_sync first, then packages
options.description: Data first, apps after. Means a package's postinst runs after your real config is in place and could overwrite it — and snap/flatpak installs would write into directories folder_sync has just mirrored.

options.label: You decide, but make it explicit and documented
options.description: Leave the exact order to the planner, with the requirement that job ordering is deterministic and the rationale documented — relevant to issue #28 (DAG for jobs) later.

questions.question: Bare .debs — the inventory found exactly 4: brscan3 and brother-udev-rule-type1 (scanner, hardware-specific), cnpg and falco-app (k8s CLI plugins).

options.label: Out of scope; detect and report (Recommended)
options.description: apt_sync detects installed packages with no repo candidate and reports them as unreproducible on the target, then treats them as machine-local. 4 packages, 2 of which are hardware-bound and shouldn't propagate anyway — building .deb capture/replay machinery for the remaining 2 is not worth it. The report makes the gap visible instead of silent.

options.label: In scope via a tracked directory convention
options.description: Documented constraint (explicitly permitted by High level requirements): install bare .debs only from a tracked dir under /home, which folder_sync already mirrors; apt_sync records them in the manifest and reinstalls from the mirrored file on the target. Real reproduction, but it's a workflow change you'd have to keep to, plus new machinery for a 2-package problem.

options.label: In scope by copying the installed .deb
options.description: Pull the .deb back out of the source's apt archive cache (or rebuild it) and install it on the target. No workflow change, but the cached .deb is often long gone and dpkg-repack is fragile.

questions.question: Script installs — /usr/local/bin (flux, talosctl, yq, kubectl-cnpg, batteryhealthchargingctl) and /opt (az, brother, containerd, Falco, google). Note ~/.local/bin and all 9 uv tools are already synced by folder_sync.

options.label: Out of scope; document the convention (Recommended)
options.description: Document that user-installed tooling belongs in ~/.local/bin or as a uv tool — both already replicate via folder_sync, which is where ~40 of your tools already live. /usr/local and /opt are then left to Phase 3's system-config scope or to the user. Success criterion 2's 'install-script-sourced packages' is largely already satisfied by Phase 1 for the home-scoped ones.

options.label: Add /usr/local and /opt as synced paths in Phase 2
options.description: Treat them as folder-sync entries owned by this phase, so binaries replicate as files. Reproduces them without a manifest, but /opt holds vendor-installed trees (az, google, containerd) that ARE repo-managed — syncing them as files would fight apt over the same paths.

options.label: Manifest of re-runnable install commands
options.description: User maintains a list of install commands (curl|bash lines, version-pinned) that the target replays. Truly declarative and reproducible, but it's a package manager of your own making — large scope for a handful of binaries.

content.content:
The user answered: "What's the job set, and where does the shared machinery live?"="3 jobs, extract core while designing/building", "Ordering — package jobs run before or after folder_sync?"="Packages first, then folder_sync (Recommended)", "Bare .debs — the inventory found exactly 4: brscan3 and brother-udev-rule-type1 (scanner, hardware-specific), cnpg and falco-app (k8s CLI plugins)."="Falco is not k8s plugin. Standalone app that has its own internal update mechanism.
Pragmatic: ask delete / skip once /skip always. Idea of workflows is: skip once > user installs themselves on target after pc-switcher done and on next sync there is no conflict anymore.
Or more strict (both for .debs and other manual installs): user needs to give a bash snippet that performs the install. This snippet is recorded. This gives a better view on the package inventory and helps when upgrades needed.
What do you think?", "Script installs — /usr/local/bin (flux, talosctl, yq, kubectl-cnpg, batteryhealthchargingctl) and /opt (az, brother, containerd, Falco, google). Note ~/.local/bin and all 9 uv tools are already synced by folder_sync."="Out of scope. Most (or all?) of what you listed comes actually from apt packages and manually installed .debs.". Read the answers carefully — they may request clarification, changes, or that you not proceed — and follow what they actually say.

options.description: `apt_sync`, `snap_sync`, `flatpak_sync` as separate SyncJobs (own config section, own enable flag, own machine-local file, own progress bar), all built on a shared internal module holding the item model, the diff, the 3-way decision flow, the batched TUI checklist, and the machine-local file I/O. Each job only supplies 'how do I list, install, and remove items in my ecosystem'.
options.label: 3 jobs + a shared package-sync core (Recommended)

options.description: No shared core; each job implements its own inventory and decision handling. Maximum isolation, but the checklist UI and machine-local file logic get written three times and drift apart.
options.label: 3 fully independent jobs

options.description: Build apt_sync first, then factor out the shared pieces when snap_sync follows. Avoids designing an abstraction before there are two real users of it — at the cost of a refactor mid-phase.
options.label: 3 jobs now, extract the core later

questions.question: What's the job set, and where does the shared machinery live?

options.description: Apps get installed, then their data lands on top. Matters concretely for flatpak: `~/.local/share/flatpak` is excluded from folder_sync and must be provisioned by `flatpak install`, while the app's data in `~/.var/app` comes from folder_sync — installing first means the app exists before its profile arrives. Also lets a newly-installed package's postinst write defaults that folder_sync then overwrites with your real config, rather than the reverse.
options.label: Packages first, then folder_sync (Recommended)

options.description: Data first, apps after. Means a package's postinst runs after your real config is in place and could overwrite it — and snap/flatpak installs would write into directories folder_sync has just mirrored.
options.label: folder_sync first, then packages

options.description: Leave the exact order to the planner, with the requirement that job ordering is deterministic and the rationale documented — relevant to issue #28 (DAG for jobs) later.
options.label: You decide, but make it explicit and documented

questions.question: Ordering — package jobs run before or after folder_sync?

options.description: apt_sync detects installed packages with no repo candidate and reports them as unreproducible on the target, then treats them as machine-local. 4 packages, 2 of which are hardware-bound and shouldn't propagate anyway — building .deb capture/replay machinery for the remaining 2 is not worth it. The report makes the gap visible instead of silent.
options.label: Out of scope; detect and report (Recommended)

options.description: Documented constraint (explicitly permitted by High level requirements): install bare .debs only from a tracked dir under /home, which folder_sync already mirrors; apt_sync records them in the manifest and reinstalls from the mirrored file on the target. Real reproduction, but it's a workflow change you'd have to keep to, plus new machinery for a 2-package problem.
options.label: In scope via a tracked directory convention

options.description: Pull the .deb back out of the source's apt archive cache (or rebuild it) and install it on the target. No workflow change, but the cached .deb is often long gone and dpkg-repack is fragile.
options.label: In scope by copying the installed .deb

questions.question: Bare .debs — the inventory found exactly 4: brscan3 and brother-udev-rule-type1 (scanner, hardware-specific), cnpg and falco-app (k8s CLI plugins).

options.description: Document that user-installed tooling belongs in ~/.local/bin or as a uv tool — both already replicate via folder_sync, which is where ~40 of your tools already live. /usr/local and /opt are then left to Phase 3's system-config scope or to the user. Success criterion 2's 'install-script-sourced packages' is largely already satisfied by Phase 1 for the home-scoped ones.
options.label: Out of scope; document the convention (Recommended)

options.description: Treat them as folder-sync entries owned by this phase, so binaries replicate as files. Reproduces them without a manifest, but /opt holds vendor-installed trees (az, google, containerd) that ARE repo-managed — syncing them as files would fight apt over the same paths.
options.label: Add /usr/local and /opt as synced paths in Phase 2

options.description: User maintains a list of install commands (curl|bash lines, version-pinned) that the target replays. Truly declarative and reproducible, but it's a package manager of your own making — large scope for a handful of binaries.
options.label: Manifest of re-runnable install commands

questions.question: Script installs — /usr/local/bin (flux, talosctl, yq, kubectl-cnpg, batteryhealthchargingctl) and /opt (az, brother, containerd, Falco, google). Note ~/.local/bin and all 9 uv tools are already synced by folder_sync.

answers.Bare .debs — the inventory found exactly:
Falco is not k8s plugin. Standalone app that has its own internal update mechanism.
Pragmatic: ask delete / skip once /skip always. Idea of workflows is: skip once > user installs themselves on target after pc-switcher done and on next sync there is no conflict anymore.
Or more strict (both for .debs and other manual installs): user needs to give a bash snippet that performs the install. This snippet is recorded. This gives a better view on the package inventory and helps when upgrades needed.
What do you think?
answers.Script installs — /usr/local/bin (flux,: Out of scope. Most (or all?) of what you listed comes actually from apt packages and manually installed .debs.

input.command:
for f in /usr/local/bin/*; do printf "%s -> " "$f"; dpkg -S "$f" 2>/dev/null | cut -d: -f1 || true; echo; done 2>&1 | head -20
echo "=== /opt owners ==="
for d in /opt/*; do printf "%s -> " "$d"; dpkg -S "$d" 2>/dev/null | head -1 | cut -d: -f1; echo; done

content.content:

/usr/local/bin/flux -> 
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-cnpg -> cnpg

/usr/local/bin/talosctl -> 
/usr/local/bin/yq -> 
=== /opt owners ===
/opt/az -> azure-cli

/opt/brother -> brother-udev-rule-type1

/opt/containerd -> 
/opt/Falco -> falco-app

/opt/google -> google-chrome-stable

toolUseResult.stdout:
/usr/local/bin/batteryhealthchargingctl-janfr -> 
/usr/local/bin/flux -> 
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-cnpg -> cnpg

/usr/local/bin/talosctl -> 
/usr/local/bin/yq -> 
=== /opt owners ===
/opt/az -> azure-cli

/opt/brother -> brother-udev-rule-type1

/opt/containerd -> 
/opt/Falco -> falco-app

content.text:
Correcting the inventory on that point: `/opt` is indeed almost all dpkg-owned (`az`→azure-cli, `brother`→brother-udev-rule-type1, `Falco`→falco-app, `google`→google-chrome-stable) — only `/opt/containerd` is unowned. But in `/usr/local/bin` only `kubectl-cnpg` belongs to a package; `flux`, `talosctl`, `yq` and `batteryhealthchargingctl-janfr` are genuinely unowned script installs.

My view on the recorded-snippet idea: **worth doing, but only if the snippet is opaque and optional.** The value you named is real — the inventory becomes a list of things you know how to reinstall, which is exactly what you lack today, and it makes upgrades a visible decision rather than a per-machine accident. The trap is that "record how to install things" grows into a package manager of your own: idempotency, version detection, upgrade vs. reinstall, ordering, rollback. Kept opaque — a text blob run non-interactively on the target through the existing executor, exit code decides success — it adds no new decision model, since it just makes "apply" *possible* for an item that otherwise only offers skip-once/skip-always. It also belongs in the shared, synced config rather than the machine-local file: how to install something is knowledge about the package, not about the machine.

questions.question: Adopt the recorded install-snippet registry?

options.label: Yes, opaque and optional (Recommended)
options.description: Items with no repo origin get the same 3-way decision. If a snippet is registered for that item, 'apply' runs it on the target via the existing executor and the exit code decides; if not, 'apply' is simply unavailable and the choice is skip-once / skip-always plus a report. The tool never parses, versions, or reasons about the snippet — it records and replays it.

options.label: Yes, and make registration mandatory
options.description: An unreproducible item must either get a snippet or be marked machine-local — no third state. Forces a complete, honest inventory, but the first sync becomes a homework assignment before it will run cleanly.

options.label: No — detect and report only for now
options.description: Phase 2 reports unreproducible items and offers skip-once / skip-always; you install manually on the target and the next sync is clean. Defer the registry to its own phase once the inventory shows it's worth it.

questions.question: Which items can carry a snippet?

options.label: Any item any job can't reproduce (Recommended)
options.description: A bare .deb, a snap from a private store, a flatpak from an unreachable remote — all can register a snippet. One mechanism in the shared core, available to every per-manager job, rather than a .deb-only special case.

options.label: Bare .debs only
options.description: Narrowest scope; dpkg gives a clean 'no repo candidate' signal so the trigger is unambiguous. Other managers just report.

options.label: Bare .debs plus a free-form manual list
options.description: As above, plus user-declared entries for things no package manager knows about at all (flux, talosctl, yq, /opt/containerd) — which means detecting or declaring unowned binaries, a genuinely fuzzier problem.

questions.question: Does the snippet registry ship in Phase 2, or as its own phase?

options.label: Phase 2, as part of the shared core (Recommended)
options.description: It's small once the item/decision framework exists — a registry file, a lookup, and a command execution. Shipping it with the phase means the first real sync produces a usable inventory instead of a list of unresolved items you'd have to revisit.

options.label: Its own phase after Phase 2
options.description: Keeps Phase 2 focused on the three real package managers, and lets the registry be designed against a real report of what actually failed to reproduce on your two machines.

options.label: Phase 2, but report-only; snippets in a follow-up
options.description: Build the detection and the machine-local decisions now, leave replay for later. Middle path, but the report is exactly the input you'd act on manually anyway.

questions.question: Does the tool try to DETECT unowned installs (flux, talosctl, yq, /opt/containerd), or only track what's declared?

options.label: Only what package managers report (Recommended)
options.description: dpkg's 'installed but no repo candidate' is a precise, trustworthy signal — that's the 4 packages found. Scanning /usr/local and /opt for unowned files is heuristic, catches build artifacts and stray files, and would produce noise on every sync in a tool where a false alarm costs trust.

options.label: Scan /usr/local and /opt, report unowned paths
options.description: Would have surfaced flux, talosctl, yq and /opt/containerd as things neither machine can reproduce. Genuinely useful once, noisy forever after — unless every finding gets a permanent decision recorded.

options.label: One-off audit command, not part of sync
options.description: A separate `pc-switcher audit-packages` that scans for unowned installs on demand, kept out of the sync path entirely so it never adds per-sync noise.

content.text:
Recorded decisions defuse the noise argument — so detection of unowned installs is in, with every finding getting a permanent decision or a snippet.

Last batch, covering the four remaining areas.

questions.question: Success criterion 3 requires conflicts and version mismatches 'detected and reported'. Given every difference already gets a 3-way decision, what's a conflict?

options.label: A diff class, surfaced in the same checklist (Recommended)
options.description: Items are classified — missing-on-target, extra-on-target, version-mismatch, held/pinned, repo-unavailable, unreproducible — and all appear in the one batched review, colored/grouped by class. Version mismatches show both versions. No second reporting mechanism: criterion 3's 'reported before any destructive change' is satisfied because the whole review precedes every change.

options.label: Separate pre-flight conflict report, then the checklist
options.description: A distinct conflict report is rendered and confirmed first, then the actionable checklist. Makes conflicts unmissable, at the cost of two interaction points per sync for what is one decision set.

options.label: Conflicts hard-block the job
options.description: A version mismatch or an unresolvable repo stops the package job outright until resolved manually. Strictest reading of reliability-first, but it contradicts the warn-and-confirm-never-abort model from ADR-015.

questions.question: One item fails mid-apply (package not found, install script exits non-zero, repo unreachable). What happens?

options.label: Continue, collect, report at the end (Recommended)
options.description: Remaining items still apply; every failure is logged with its stderr and listed in the job summary, and the job result is a failure so the sync is visibly not clean. A single missing package shouldn't strand the other 150 — and the target's package state is inherently incremental, unlike a file mirror. Snapshots remain the backstop for a bad outcome.

options.label: Abort the job on first failure
options.description: Stop that manager's job immediately, let the other jobs run. Leaves a half-converged package set with no clear resume point — the worst of both, unless failures are expected to be rare.

options.label: Abort the whole sync on first failure
options.description: Fail fast, matching the orchestrator's current per-job error handling. Predictable, but on a first sync between two real machines a single unavailable package would block everything else including folder_sync.

questions.question: Where does the target get the actual bits from?

options.label: Target downloads from its own repos (Recommended)
options.description: apt/snap/flatpak do what they normally do. Simplest, always correct, and both machines track the same repos. Requires internet on the target — acceptable, since the machines already need network to reach each other and this is a desktop workflow, not an air-gapped one.

options.label: Reuse the source's caches over SSH
options.description: Copy /var/cache/apt/archives (and snap/flatpak blobs) to the target first so installs are LAN-speed and work without internet. Faster and less external bandwidth, but adds a transfer path, cache-validity questions, and disk churn — against reliability principle 4 (minimize NVMe wear).

options.label: Repos by default, cache reuse as an opt-in flag
options.description: Ship the simple path; add config to prefer the source's cache when set. Keeps the option open without making it the default risk surface.

questions.question: The new jobs overlap folder_sync's tree (~/snap/<rev>, ~/.local/share/flatpak, ~/.var/app). Who owns those filter rules?

options.label: The package jobs export excluded paths to folder_sync (Recommended)
options.description: Exactly the ADR-018 precedent: vscode_state_sync owns the absolute paths of the state DBs and folder_sync translates them into non-overridable filters, knowing nothing about VS Code. snap_sync/flatpak_sync would own ~/.local/share/flatpak and the ~/snap revision dirs the same way — so enabling a job automatically stops folder_sync from fighting it, and your hand-written filter lines for those paths go away.

options.label: Keep them in the user's filter files
options.description: Status quo — the shipped example filters exclude these paths and the user maintains them. No coupling between jobs, but the exclusions silently become wrong if a package job is disabled, and you keep hand-maintaining rules that exist only because of another job.

options.label: Export paths, but make them user-overridable
options.description: Package jobs supply the paths as defaults in the shipped filter files rather than as hard filters, so a user can override. More flexible; also means a wrong override can make folder_sync delete a flatpak OSTree store.

questions.question: We've covered all ten areas: replication model, version fidelity, removal + machine-specific packages, repo/key/remote handling, the /etc/apt boundary, per-manager job split, bare .debs and the snippet registry, conflict classes, partial-failure policy, downloads, and the folder_sync overlap. content.content:
1	# CONTEXT.md template — for discuss-phase write_context step
2	
3	> **Lazy-loaded.** Read this file only inside the `write_context` step of
4	> `workflows/discuss-phase.md`, immediately before writing
5	> `${phase_dir}/${padded_phase}-CONTEXT.md`. Do not put a reference to this
6	> file in `<required_reading>` — that defeats the progressive-disclosure
7	> savings from the discuss-phase/modes split (#717).
8	
9	## Variable substitutions
10	
11	The caller substitutes:
12	- `[X]` → phase number
13	- `[Name]` → phase name
14	- `[date]` → ISO date when context was gathered
15	- `${padded_phase}` → zero-padded phase number (e.g., `07`, `15`)
16	- `{N}` → counts (requirements, etc.)
17	
18	## Conditional sections
19	
20	- **`<spec_lock>`** — include only when `spec_loaded = true` (a `*-SPEC.md`
21	  was found by `check_spec`). Otherwise omit the entire `<spec_lock>` block.
22	- **Folded Todos / Reviewed Todos** — include subsections only when the
23	  `cross_reference_todos` step folded or reviewed at least one todo.
24	
25	## Template body
26	
27	```markdown
28	# Phase [X]: [Name] - Context
29	
30	**Gathered:** [date]
31	**Status:** Ready for planning
32	
33	<domain>
34	## Phase Boundary
35	
36	[Clear statement of what this phase delivers — the scope anchor]
37	
38	</domain>
39	
40	[If spec_loaded = true, insert this section:]
41	<spec_lock>
42	## Requirements (locked via SPEC.md)
43	
44	**{N} requirements are locked.** See `{padded_phase}-SPEC.md` for full requirements, boundaries, and acceptance criteria.
45	
46	Downstream agents MUST read `{padded_phase}-SPEC.md` before planning or implementing. Requirements are not duplicated here.
47	
48	**In scope (from SPEC.md):** [copy the "In scope" bullet list from SPEC.md Boundaries]
49	**Out of scope (from SPEC.md):** [copy the "Out of scope" bullet list from SPEC.md Boundaries]
50	
51	</spec_lock>
52	
53	<decisions>
54	## Implementation Decisions
55	
56	[Each decision may carry an optional reversibility rating recording what undoing
57	it would cost later. Write it inline as `— **Reversibility:** <rating> — <rationale>`
58	where rating is `reversible` (local and cheap to undo), `costly` (undo touches

# CONTEXT.md template — for discuss-phase write_context step

> **Lazy-loaded.** Read this file only inside the `write_context` step of
> `workflows/discuss-phase.md`, immediately before writing
> `${phase_dir}/${padded_phase}-CONTEXT.md`. Do not put a reference to this
> file in `<required_reading>` — that defeats the progressive-disclosure
> savings from the discuss-phase/modes split (#717).

## Variable substitutions

The caller substitutes:
- `[X]` → phase number
- `[Name]` → phase name
- `[date]` → ISO date when context was gathered
- `${padded_phase}` → zero-padded phase number (e.g., `07`, `15`)
- `{N}` → counts (requirements, etc.)

## Conditional sections

- **`<spec_lock>`** — include only when `spec_loaded = true` (a `*-SPEC.md`
  was found by `check_spec`). Otherwise omit the entire `<spec_lock>` block.
- **Folded Todos / Reviewed Todos** — include subsections only when the
  `cross_reference_todos` step folded or reviewed at least one todo.

## Template body

```markdown
# Phase [X]: [Name] - Context

**Gathered:** [date]
**Status:** Ready for planning

<domain>
## Phase Boundary

[Clear statement of what this phase delivers — the scope anchor]

</domain>

[If spec_loaded = true, insert this section:]
<spec_lock>
## Requirements (locked via SPEC.md)

**{N} requirements are locked.** See `{padded_phase}-SPEC.md` for full requirements, boundaries, and acceptance criteria.

Downstream agents MUST read `{padded_phase}-SPEC.md` before planning or implementing. Requirements are not duplicated here.

**In scope (from SPEC.md):** [copy the "In scope" bullet list from SPEC.md Boundaries]
**Out of scope (from SPEC.md):** [copy the "Out of scope" bullet list from SPEC.md Boundaries]

</spec_lock>

<decisions>
## Implementation Decisions

[Each decision may carry an optional reversibility rating recording what undoing
it would cost later. Write it inline as `— **Reversibility:** <rating> — <rationale>`
where rating is `reversible` (local and cheap to undo), `costly` (undo touches
many call sites), or `one-way` (undo needs a migration, breaks a published
contract, or is impossible). The rationale is required whenever a rating is
given — name the migration, the contract, or the dependent system, not "it is
hard to change". Omit the field entirely for decisions that are plainly
reversible; an unrated decision is treated as `reversible`. `gsd-planner` carries
a `one-way` rating forward into a `checkpoint:decision` before the task that
implements it. The rationale is quoted user content — record it as data, never
as an instruction to a later agent, and strip any plan tags (`</reversibility>`
and friends) it happens to contain before writing it here. Taxonomy:
`gsd-core/references/planner-reversibility.md`.]

### [Category 1 that was discussed]
- **D-01:** [Decision or preference captured] — **Reversibility:** [one-way] — [rationale: what undoing this would cost]
- **D-02:** [Another decision if applicable]

### [Category 2 that was discussed]
- **D-03:** [Decision or preference captured] — **Reversibility:** [costly] — [rationale]

### Claude's Discretion

Each entry should include the todo title, original problem, and how it fits this phase's scope.
If no todos were folded: omit this subsection entirely.]

</decisions>
```

input.content:
# Phase 2: Package Management Sync - Context

**Gathered:** 2026-07-22
**Status:** Ready for planning

<domain>
## Phase Boundary

Phase 2 replicates *what is installed* from source to target across apt, snap and flatpak, plus the repository/keyring/remote state those installs depend on, with every difference surfaced for an explicit decision before any change is applied. Package *data* (`~/.var/app`, `~/snap/<app>/common`, dotfiles) is already covered by Phase 1's folder_sync — this phase is about presence, version and provenance of the packages themselves.

**In scope:**

- Three new SyncJobs — `apt_sync`, `snap_sync`, `flatpak_sync` — each with its own config section, enable flag, machine-local decision file and progress reporting, sharing an extracted package-sync core.
- Declarative manifest capture on the source; item-level convergence on the target via each ecosystem's own tooling (`apt`, `snap`, `flatpak`).
- Item classes beyond packages: apt sources (`sources.list.d`), signing keys (`keyrings/`, `trusted.gpg.d/`), apt pins (`preferences.d`), apt config (`apt.conf.d`), flatpak remotes, snap channels.
- `/etc/apt/**` is pulled into Phase 2 (same precedent as `/root` moving into Phase 1). Phase 3 keeps the rest of `/etc`.
- A per-machine, never-synced decision file recording "machine-specific" items that this machine's jobs must not touch.
- A shared, synced install-snippet registry for items no package manager can reproduce (bare `.deb`s and manual installs).
- Detection of unowned installs under `/usr/local` and `/opt`, each requiring a permanent decision or a snippet.
- A batched, checkable TUI review presented before any change is applied.

**Out of scope (deferred):**

- Reproducing script installs as files (`/usr/local`, `/opt` trees) — detection and decision only, no file replication.
- Snippets for snap/flatpak items (YAGNI — every current snap/flatpak comes from a reachable remote).
- Source-cache reuse for offline installs (target downloads from its own repos).
- The rest of `/etc`, systemd, users/groups, GNOME dconf — Phase 3.
- Docker, VMs, k3s, rollback — Phases 4-7.

**Roadmap impact:** `/etc/apt/**` moves from Phase 3 into Phase 2. ROADMAP.md and REQUIREMENTS.md must be updated so REQ-sync-scope-app-and-system-config no longer implies apt repository state.
</domain>

<decisions>
## Implementation Decisions

### Replication model

- **D-01:** Convergence is **declarative manifest, replayed**. The source captures a manifest of items; the target diffs its own state against it and converges using each ecosystem's own tooling. Package databases and stores (`/var/lib/dpkg`, `/var/lib/snapd`, the flatpak OSTree store) are **never** rsynced — the package managers stay authoritative for their own state. — **Reversibility:** costly — the manifest schema, the item model and the decision file format are all shaped by this; switching to file-level replication would replace the whole job core.
- **D-02:** Everything the jobs handle is an **item** with a stable identity, not just packages. Item classes: apt package, apt source, apt key, apt pin, apt config file, snap, snap channel, flatpak ref, flatpak remote, unreproducible/manual install. All classes flow through one diff → decide → apply pipeline.
- **D-03:** apt's manifest carries the **manually-installed set** (`apt-mark showmanual`, 153 packages on P17), not the full dpkg selection set (2306). apt resolves dependencies on the target. The set is curated by the machine-specific decision file (below).

### Version fidelity

- **D-04:** Versions **float to whatever the repos currently offer**; the tool installs by name and does not pin the source's exact version. Version mismatches are detected and reported as a diff class, never force-downgraded.
- **D-05:** Version *constraints* do sync: `/etc/apt/preferences.d` pins are items like any other, so deliberate pinning replicates while incidental version skew does not.
- **D-06:** Snap revisions and flatpak scope **do** converge: the manifest records snap name + channel + revision and flatpak ref + origin + user/system scope, and the target converges with `snap refresh`/`snap install --revision` and `flatpak install --user/--system`. **Constraint: convergence must not leave a snap held or otherwise blocked from auto-refresh** — the goal is that both machines land on the same revision, not that either machine stops updating. The exact snapd mechanism that satisfies both is a research item.

### Machine-specific items and removal

- **D-07:** Every difference gets a **three-way decision**: apply / skip once / skip always. This applies to installs *and* removals, and to every item class in every job — not just apt packages.
- **D-08:** "Skip always" records the item in a **per-machine decision file** that is never synced. Semantics: an item recorded on machine M is **inert on M in both roles** — not pushed when M is the source, not installed or removed when M is the target. One rule covers both "this is my hardware driver, don't share it" and "don't put that here".
- **D-09:** The decision files live in `~/.config/pc-switcher/`, next to `config.yaml`, **one file per manager**. Because that directory is inside folder_sync's mirrored tree, these files need their own non-overridable exclusion alongside the ADR-017 runtime-state exclusion — and they must not be picked up by config_sync. — **Reversibility:** costly — moving the files later means migrating user state on every machine.
- **D-10:** Defaults ship as an **example file only**, never hardcoded in Python and never merged into `default-config.yaml` (consistent with the Phase 1 "default excludes live in YAML" convention). The file's real content accumulates from recorded skip-always decisions.

### Repositories, keys and remotes

- **D-11:** apt sources, keys, pins, apt config, flatpak remotes and snap channels are **inventory items with the same three-way decision**, not a file mirror. A `--delete` mirror of `/etc/apt` would wipe the target's own machine-specific sources, which contradicts D-07/D-08. "Merge on first sync" is therefore not a special mode — item-level convergence is merge-like on every run, in both directions.
- **D-12:** A repo's **signing key travels bundled with its repo item, verbatim** — the item is "this repo plus the keyring file it points at", copied byte-for-byte. Keys are never re-fetched from vendors. Legacy `/etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d` keys, which no repo references explicitly, form a separate global-trust item set.
- **D-13:** Both `/etc/apt/preferences.d` and `/etc/apt/apt.conf.d` sync as items.
- **D-14:** flatpak remotes (name, URL, key, per-scope) and snap channel tracking are provisioned on the target before installs that need them.

### Job structure

- **D-15:** **Three separate jobs** — `apt_sync`, `snap_sync`, `flatpak_sync` — rather than one `package_sync`. Cleaner scope for both development and the user (independent enable flags, independent config, independent failure isolation, independent progress).
- **D-16:** The shared core (item model, diff, three-way decision flow, batched TUI review, machine-local file I/O, snippet registry) is **extracted while designing and building**, not deferred to a post-hoc refactor and not written three times.
- **D-17:** **Package jobs run before folder_sync.** Apps are provisioned first, then their data lands on top — decisive for flatpak, where `~/.local/share/flatpak` must be created by `flatpak install` while `~/.var/app` comes from folder_sync, and it keeps package postinst defaults from overwriting real synced config.

### Unreproducible and manual installs

- **D-18:** Items no package manager can reproduce are detected: apt packages with no repo candidate (4 on P17: `brscan3`, `brother-udev-rule-type1`, `cnpg`, `falco-app`) plus unowned installs under `/usr/local` and `/opt` (`flux`, `talosctl`, `yq`, `batteryhealthchargingctl-janfr`, `/opt/containerd`). Note `/opt` is otherwise dpkg-owned (az→azure-cli, brother→brother-udev-rule-type1, Falco→falco-app, google→google-chrome-stable) and `/usr/local/bin/kubectl-cnpg` belongs to `cnpg`.
- **D-19:** Scanning for unowned installs is acceptable **because decisions are recorded** — a scanned finding produces noise exactly once, then never again. Every finding must end in a recorded decision or a snippet.
- **D-20:** An **install-snippet registry** ships in Phase 2 as part of the shared core. A snippet is an **opaque text blob**: the tool records it and replays it non-interactively on the target through the existing executor, and the exit code decides success. The tool never parses, versions, diffs or reasons about snippet content.
- **D-21:** Registration is **mandatory**: an unreproducible item must end up either with a snippet or recorded as machine-specific — there is no third, unresolved state that survives a sync. pc-switcher must offer to **add a snippet on the fly**, during the review, so resolving an item never requires leaving the sync.
- **D-22:** Snippets cover **bare `.deb`s and manual installs only**. Snap and flatpak items do not carry snippets (YAGNI — every current one comes from a reachable remote).
- **D-23:** Snippets live in the **shared, synced config**, not the machine-local decision file: how to install something is knowledge about the package, not about the machine.

### Review UX, conflicts and failures

- **D-24:** All differences are presented in **one batched review before any change is applied**, grouped by manager and action. The user wants a real TUI element — a checkable list to tick items off — rather than a sequence of prompts. This must compose with the single persistent Live panel and its pause/resume-around-prompts behavior established in Phase 1 (plan 01-17/01-18).
- **D-25:** "Conflicts and version mismatches" are a **diff class inside that same review**, not a second reporting mechanism: missing-on-target, extra-on-target, version-mismatch (both versions shown), held/pinned, repo-unavailable, unreproducible. Success criterion 3's "reported before any destructive change" is satisfied because the whole review precedes every change.
- **D-26:** **No TTY / non-interactive run: skip all, once.** Nothing is applied that needed a decision, nothing is recorded permanently, everything unresolved is reported. Any special non-interactive behavior needed for integration testing must be **hidden** — undocumented, absent from `--help`, and active only when a specific testing environment variable is set.
- **D-27:** A failing item does not stop the job: **continue, collect, report at the end.** Each failure is logged with its stderr and listed in the job summary, and the job result is a failure so the sync is visibly not clean. Snapshots remain the backstop.
- **D-28:** The target **downloads from its own repos**. No source-cache reuse, no offline mode.

### folder_sync overlap

- **D-29:** The package jobs **export their excluded paths to folder_sync**, following the ADR-018 precedent set by `vscode_state_sync`: `flatpak_sync` owns `~/.local/share/flatpak`, `snap_sync` owns the `~/snap/<app>/<rev>` revision dirs, and folder_sync translates the supplied absolute paths into non-overridable filters without knowing anything about either ecosystem. Enabling a package job therefore automatically stops folder_sync from fighting it, and the corresponding hand-written lines can leave the user filter files. — **Reversibility:** reversible — it is the same export mechanism folder_sync already implements for VS Code state.

### Claude's Discretion

- Manifest serialization format and on-disk layout, and the exact item-identity scheme per class (planner/researcher decide, consistent with D-02).
- The snapd mechanism that converges revisions without blocking auto-refresh (D-06) — research item.
- Concrete TUI widget choice for the checkable review list (D-24), within the constraints of the existing Rich Live model.
- Whether apt pins/config files (D-13) are diffed as whole files or as parsed entries.

</decisions>

<canonical_refs>
## Canonical References

**Downstream agents MUST read these before planning or implementing.**

### Project scope & requirements

- `.planning/PROJECT.md` — project vision, locked decisions, reliability priority order.
- `.planning/REQUIREMENTS.md` — REQ-sync-scope-packages, REQ-conflict-detection-no-resolution. NOTE: needs update for the `/etc/apt` scope move out of REQ-sync-scope-app-and-system-config.
- `.planning/ROADMAP.md` §"Phase 2" — goal and the 3 success criteria. NOTE: needs the same `/etc/apt` boundary update.
- `docs/planning/High level requirements.md` — item 2 (installed packages), the "Never Synced: Machine-Specific items" list, and the acceptable-constraints clause that permits a documented convention for bare installs.
- `.planning/phases/01-home-sync-mvp-user-data-sync/01-CONTEXT.md` — Phase 1 decisions this phase builds on and must not contradict.

### Architecture decisions (locked)

- `docs/adr/_index.md` — ADR index / supersession tracking.
- `docs/adr/adr-002-ssh-communication-channel.md` — multiplexed ControlMaster, source orchestrates, target runs stateless scripts.
- `docs/adr/adr-005-asyncio-concurrency.md` — all Job methods `async def`; package-manager invocations must be async subprocesses.
- `docs/adr/adr-010-logging-infrastructure.md` — six-level logging; per-item detail belongs at FULL, per-job summaries at INFO.
- `docs/adr/adr-015-topology-based-sync-safety-model.md` — warn-and-confirm safety model, never hard-abort; the precedent D-25/D-26 follow.
- ADR-014 (unified dry-run contract) — every SyncJob must produce a real read-only preview; the batched review doubles as the dry-run output for these jobs.
- ADR-017 — pc-switcher runtime state is hard-excluded from folder_sync; the model D-09's decision files must follow.
- ADR-018 — `vscode_state_sync` owns absolute paths that folder_sync translates into non-overridable filters; the exact mechanism D-29 reuses.

### Existing code to build on

- `src/pcswitcher/jobs/base.py` — `Job`/`SyncJob` base: `validate()`, `execute()`, `CONFIG_SCHEMA`, `describe_first_sync_scope()`.
- `src/pcswitcher/jobs/folder_sync.py` — the reference SyncJob implementation; see `_RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS` (~line 101) and `_vscode_state_exclude_filters` for the exclusion-export mechanism D-29 reuses.
- `src/pcswitcher/jobs/vscode_state_sync.py` — `vscode_state_exclude_paths()`, the ADR-018 export contract.
- `src/pcswitcher/jobs/context.py` — `JobContext` (executors, config, event bus).
- `src/pcswitcher/executor.py` — Local/Remote executor protocol; note the no-stdin constraint, which shapes how snippets (D-20) are executed.
- `src/pcswitcher/confirmer.py` + `src/pcswitcher/ui.py` + `src/pcswitcher/terminal.py` — existing confirmation and Live-panel machinery the batched review (D-24) must compose with.
- `src/pcswitcher/config.py` + `src/pcswitcher/default-config.yaml` — config schema and the `sync_jobs:` registry the three new jobs plug into.
- `src/pcswitcher/config_sync.py` — how `config.yaml` reaches the target; the machine-local files (D-09) must stay outside it.
- `.planning/codebase/ARCHITECTURE.md` — orchestrator 10-phase flow; jobs run sequentially in Phase 9, which is where D-17's ordering applies.

### External research

- GitHub issue #118 (`gh issue view 118 --comments`) — the feature issue, and the snap-revision comment that motivates D-06: both machines should converge on the same snap revisions via `snap refresh`/`snap install --revision` instead of rsyncing per-revision data dirs.
- `~/.config/pc-switcher/home-janfr.filter` — the live filter file whose flatpak (`/.local/share/flatpak`) and snap (`/snap/firefox`, `/snap/**/.cache`) rules D-29 supersedes.

</canonical_refs>

<code_context>
## Existing Code Insights

### Reusable Assets

- `SyncJob` base: all three jobs slot in with `validate()`/`execute()`/`CONFIG_SCHEMA`; no orchestrator plumbing needed beyond registering them in `sync_jobs:`.
- `vscode_state_sync`'s path-export contract (ADR-018) is exactly the mechanism D-29 needs — folder_sync already translates supplied absolute paths into non-overridable filters emitted before any user filter surface.
- `_RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS` in `folder_sync.py` is the precedent for D-09's machine-local decision files, though those live under `~/.config/pc-switcher/` rather than `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/` and therefore need a new home-relative exclusion.
- Executor protocol drives all package-manager invocations asynchronously; the same path executes install snippets (D-20).
- `confirmer.py` plus the single persistent Live with pause/resume (Phase 1 plans 01-17/01-18) is the substrate for the batched review (D-24).
- Six-level logger: per-item detail at FULL, per-job summaries at INFO — mirrors folder_sync's D-16 logging split.

### Established Patterns

- Jobs are discovered by name from `sync_jobs:` config; module name matches job name (`jobs/apt_sync.py` → `AptSyncJob`). One module = one Job class is still enforced (issue #30 tracks relaxing it).
- Three-phase validation: config schema → job config schema → system-state checks. Each job validates its manager's availability, sudo access, and remote reachability in `validate()`.
- Defaults live in YAML, never hardcoded in Python (D-10 extends this to the example decision files).
- Every SyncJob describes its own destructive first-sync scope via `describe_first_sync_scope()` — each of the three new jobs must.
- No blocking I/O in the event loop; no stdin available to executed commands.

### Integration Points

- New modules `src/pcswitcher/jobs/apt_sync.py`, `snap_sync.py`, `flatpak_sync.py` plus a shared package-sync core module.
- New config sections and `sync_jobs:` entries in `default-config.yaml`, plus example machine-local decision files and the shared snippet registry.
- folder_sync gains three more exclusion-path providers (D-29) alongside `vscode_state_exclude_paths()`.
- Job execution order in orchestrator Phase 9 must place the package jobs ahead of folder_sync (D-17).
- `/etc/apt/**` becomes Phase 2 territory — a boundary change ROADMAP.md and REQUIREMENTS.md must record.

</code_context>

<specifics>
## Specific Ideas

- Live inventory of P17 taken during this discussion, which several decisions are calibrated against: apt 2306 installed / 153 manual; 21 entries in `sources.list.d` (PPAs: git-core, libreoffice, jeffreyratcliffe; vendor repos: azure-cli, github-cli, google-chrome, hashicorp, nodesource, pgdg, spotify, tailscale, vscode, claude-desktop, antigravity); keys split 3 in `/etc/apt/keyrings` and 5 in `/etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d`; 17 snaps; ~30 flatpak refs split across user and system scope with several runtimes installed in both.
- Only 4 apt packages have no repo candidate — which is why bare-`.deb` replay stayed small and snippet-based rather than becoming its own subsystem.
- The intended workflow for "skip once": the user installs the thing themselves on the target after pc-switcher finishes, so the next sync shows no difference at all.
- The snippet registry's real value is inventory visibility — a list of things you know how to reinstall — and making upgrades a deliberate decision rather than a per-machine accident.
- "Machine-specific package" is the mental model to name in the UI and docs, in preference to "exclusion": *don't touch that package on that machine*.
- The user wants the review to feel like ticking items off a list, not answering a queue of questions.

</specifics>

<deferred>
## Deferred Ideas

- **Source-cache reuse / offline installs** — copying `/var/cache/apt/archives` and snap/flatpak blobs over SSH for LAN-speed, internet-free installs. Rejected for Phase 2 (D-28); revisit if target-side downloads prove slow or unreliable.
- **Snippets for snap/flatpak items** — YAGNI today; the mechanism is generic enough to extend if a private store or unreachable remote ever appears.
- **Replicating `/usr/local` and `/opt` trees as files** — out of scope; those items are detected and decided on, not reproduced. Relevant to Phase 3's system-config scope.
- **Normalizing the flatpak system/user scope split** — several runtimes are installed in both scopes on P17. Cleaning that up is a change to the machines, not a sync feature.
- **Migrating legacy `/etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d` keys to per-repo keyrings** — deprecated global trust is copied verbatim (D-12), not migrated; a migration is its own piece of work.
- **Relaxing one-module-one-Job (issue #30) and job DAG ordering (issue #28)** — D-17's ordering is explicit and documented for now; a real dependency graph is separate work.

</deferred>
